content.text: Tool Error Messages

When a tool call fails, the error message the model receives is as important as the tool's schema. A raw Python exception with a stack trace gives the model no useful information — it cannot tell whether to retry, try different arguments, or stop and inform the user. A structured error response with a typed code, a human-readable message, and a retryable flag gives the model everything it needs to recover gracefully.

attrs.alt: Tool error response anatomy and error taxonomy
attrs.title: Tool Error Response Anatomy

content.text: Treat tool error messages as a first-class part of your tool interface. The model reads them like output — they determine the next action. Vague or raw errors produce unreliable agent behaviour.
Structuring Error Responses

Every tool should return a consistent error shape rather than raise exceptions. The minimal useful structure contains three fields: a machine-readable error code the model can branch on, a human-readable message with specific context, and a boolean retryable flag.

The error code should be a SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE string drawn from a stable vocabulary shared across all your tools. When all tools use the same codes, the model builds a reliable mental model of what each code means.
Error Categories and When to Retry
NOT_FOUND

The resource the model requested does not exist. Retrying with the same arguments will always fail. The model should either report the situation to the user or try a related identifier it discovered earlier.
VALIDATION_ERROR

The model provided an argument that failed a constraint — wrong type, out-of-range value, or a field that references a resource that doesn't exist. Include the failing field name and the constraint that was violated so the model can correct its input.
PERMISSION_DENIED
The caller does not have access to the requested resource or operation. Retrying will not help — the model should stop and inform the user that additional permissions are needed.
RATE_LIMITED and SERVICE_UNAVAILABLE
These are the only two error types where retrying is appropriate. Include a retry_after field in seconds so the model does not guess how long to wait.
Returning Errors vs Raising Exceptions

Tools must catch their own exceptions and return structured error dicts rather than letting exceptions propagate. When an exception escapes a tool handler, the Anthropic SDK turns it into a generic error message that loses all structure. The model cannot distinguish a NOT_FOUND from a SERVICE_UNAVAILABLE from a bug in your code.
Not Found vs Empty Results

A common mistake is returning NOT_FOUND when a search or list query returns zero results. Those are different things. NOT_FOUND means the specific resource with the given identifier does not exist. An empty result set is a valid successful response.
Validation with Pydantic

For tools with complex inputs, use Pydantic to validate before executing business logic. Pydantic's validation errors map directly to the VALIDATION_ERROR code and give you precise field-level information to include in the message.
CONFLICT: Duplicate and State Errors

When the requested action would create a duplicate or violates a state constraint, return CONFLICT with enough context for the model to recover — typically the id or key of the existing conflicting resource.
The model can now decide: call get_user with the existing_user_id, ask the user whether to update the existing record, or stop — rather than blindly retrying the same create call.
Testing Error Paths
Test each error category explicitly. Validate that the model receives structured JSON, that the error field matches your vocabulary, and that retryable is the correct value for each case.

import json
import pytest

def test_not_found_returns_structured_error():
    result = get_user("nonexistent-id")
    assert result["error"] == "NOT_FOUND"
    assert result["retryable"] is False
    assert "nonexistent-id" in result["message"]

def test_validation_error_includes_field():
    result = book_flight({"origin": "INVALID", "destination": "CDG",
                           "departure_date": "2025-06-01", "passengers": 1})
    assert result["error"] == "VALIDATION_ERROR"
    assert result["retryable"] is False

def test_conflict_includes_existing_id():
    db.create_user("alice@example.com", "Alice")
    result = create_user("alice@example.com", "Alice Again")
    assert result["error"] == "CONFLICT"
    assert "existing_user_id" in result.get("details", {})

def test_rate_limit_is_retryable():
    # Simulate rate limit from external API
    result = call_external_api_with_rate_limit()
    assert result["error"] == "RATE_LIMITED"
    assert result["retryable"] is True
    assert "retry_after" in result
Summary
Return structured dicts with error, message, and retryable — never let raw exceptions escape a tool handler.
Use a stable SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE error vocabulary: NOT_FOUND, VALIDATION_ERROR, PERMISSION_DENIED, RATE_LIMITED, SERVICE_UNAVAILABLE, CONFLICT.
Only RATE_LIMITED and SERVICE_UNAVAILABLE are retryable; all others require a different action.
Include a retry_after seconds field on RATE_LIMITED so the model waits the correct amount.
Distinguish NOT_FOUND (a specific id does not exist) from an empty result set (a valid search with zero matches).
For VALIDATION_ERROR, name the failing field and the constraint in the message so the model can correct its argument.
For CONFLICT, include the id of the conflicting existing resource so the model can decide how to proceed.
Test every error category with an explicit assertion that error, retryable, and message are correct.
